name: Conchobar mac Nessa
description: Conchobar mac Nessa, a pivotal figure in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology, stands as the king of Ulster, ruling from his stronghold at Emain Macha. More than a mere monarch, Conchobar embodies the ideal of the wise and just ruler, though his actions are often morally ambiguous, reflecting the complex ethics of early Irish society. He is renowned for his fosterage by Fergus mac Róich, who temporarily yielded the kingship. Conchobar's reign is marked by significant events, including the birth and exploits of the hero Cú Chulainn, who is often depicted as Conchobar's nephew or foster-son. His court is a nexus of heroic activity, populated by figures such as Cathbad the Druid and the warrior queen Medb of Connacht, against whom he wages constant war. Conchobar's character is multifaceted, demonstrating shrewd political acumen alongside bursts of impulsive behavior, solidifying his place as a central, yet flawed, hero within the Irish mythological landscape. His death, caused by a brain fragment lodged in his head for years, highlights the lasting consequences of violence and the enduring power of curses in Celtic lore.
subtitle: King of Ulster

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Cú Chulainn
keyMyths.description: When Deichtine, Conchobar's sister, mysteriously becomes pregnant, the seer Cathbad prophesies that her son will be a great hero. This son, Sétanta (later Cú Chulainn), is initially raised in the household of his foster parents, but eventually comes to serve Conchobar. Cú Chulainn's extraordinary feats and loyalty to Conchobar solidify the king's reputation, as the hero's triumphs are also seen as victories for Ulster and its ruler. This myth highlights the importance of prophecy and the close relationship between king and hero.

keyMyths.title: The Táin Bó Cúailnge (Cattle Raid of Cooley)
keyMyths.description: The Táin Bó Cúailnge is the central epic of the Ulster Cycle. Queen Medb of Connacht launches an invasion of Ulster to steal the Brown Bull of Cooley. As the Ulstermen are afflicted by a curse, only Cú Chulainn can defend the province. While Conchobar is incapacitated, Cú Chulainn single-handedly holds off Medb's forces. When Conchobar finally awakens, he rallies his warriors, leading to a climactic battle and the eventual defeat of Medb's army, though the Brown Bull is tragically killed.

keyMyths.title: Conchobar's Death
keyMyths.description: Conchobar's death is a consequence of a previous battle where he was struck in the head by a brain fragment launched from a sling. A physician healed the wound, but the fragment remained lodged in his head. Years later, upon hearing of the crucifixion of Christ, Conchobar becomes enraged and the brain fragment dislodges, killing him instantly. Some versions state his brain burst forth, causing his death. This tale exemplifies the long-lasting effects of violence and the interconnection of Irish mythology with Christian narratives.
